name: Canada Wildfires
region: Quebec, British Columbia, Nova Scotia, Alberta
headline: Canada burned. The smoke reached New York. Climate change lit the match.

quote.text: Climate change is greatly increasing the flammability of the fuel available for wildfires. A single spark, regardless of its source, can rapidly turn into a blazing inferno.
quote.attribution: Dr. Yan Boulanger, Research Scientist, Natural Resources Canada · WWA study, August 2023

attribution.method: Fire Weather Index attribution — cumulative Daily Severity Rating and 7-day maximum FWI over May-July 2023, Quebec study region
attribution.summary: Climate change made the fire-prone weather conditions that drove Canada's 2023 season at least twice as likely and 20-50% more intense. The full season's severity was at least seven times more likely. Canada experienced its warmest May-June since 1940, beating the previous record by 0.8°C.

method_explainer.what_attribution_science_is: For wildfires, attribution science studies the weather conditions that cause fires to start and spread — not the fires themselves. The key metric is the Fire Weather Index (FWI), which combines temperature, wind speed, relative humidity, and precipitation to estimate fire danger. Scientists compare FWI values in today's climate against a counterfactual world without human greenhouse gas emissions.
method_explainer.how_this_event_was_studied: WWA scientists analysed the cumulative Daily Severity Rating — which reflects accumulated fire-conducive conditions from the start of the season to end of July — across the Quebec study region. They also analysed the 7-day maximum FWI to assess peak intensity. Both observational records and climate models were used to compare fire weather in today's warmed climate against pre-industrial conditions.
method_explainer.what_confidence_means: High confidence because temperature and humidity trends — the key drivers of fire weather — are well-constrained by climate science. The relationship between warming and drying vegetation is physically robust. The specific percentage increases carry uncertainty ranges, but the direction and broad magnitude of the climate signal are clear.

human_impact.people_affected_note: Smoke affected hundreds of millions across Canada and the northeastern United States. On June 7, New York City AQI reached 341 — hazardous for all residents.
human_impact.deaths_note: Four direct deaths confirmed linked to wildfires. Health impacts from smoke exposure across North America are separately significant and not captured in this figure.
human_impact.displaced_note: Over 200,000 people evacuated at peak, the highest wildfire evacuation number in Canada since at least 1980.
human_impact.economic_loss_note: Total economic loss not yet consolidated. A single week of wildfire smoke in June 2023 was estimated to have cost Ontario over $1.2 billion CAD in health impacts alone.
human_impact.hectares_burned_note: 15 million hectares burned — more than double the previous Canadian record of 7.6 million hectares set in 1989.
human_impact.emitters_share_source: Canada share of global cumulative emissions, Our World in Data

satellite.description: MODIS active fire detections showing fire extent across Quebec and eastern Canada May-August 2023

at_risk.context: Remote Indigenous communities with no road access faced the hardest evacuation conditions and the most delayed recovery. Many had no option but to wait for air transport while fires approached.
Smoke from Canadian fires created hazardous air quality across the northeastern US for days. Children, elderly, and those with asthma or lung disease faced the greatest health risk.
Over 9,000 firefighters were deployed at peak, including international crews from Australia, New Zealand, South Africa, France and the US. The scale overwhelmed Canadian fire management capacity.

stats.label: further increase in likelihood of extreme fire seasons at +2°C
stats.delta: on top of already elevated risk today

stats.label: additional days above critical fire thresholds per year by 2100
stats.delta: in Canadian forest biomes under high emissions

stats.label: higher CO2 emissions from wildfires in 2023 vs long-term mean

key_outcomes.description: The Canadian government deployed military resources and coordinated the largest wildfire evacuation in modern Canadian history. International firefighting crews from 12 countries were brought in under bilateral agreements.

The 2023 season has prompted federal and provincial reviews of fire management capacity, Indigenous community preparedness, and early warning systems. Several provinces announced funding increases for fire prevention and prescribed burn programs.

curated_coverage.headline: Canada's 2023 wildfire season shattered every record — here's what scientists learned
Climate change made Canada's record wildfires at least twice as likely, study finds
Indigenous communities say they were left behind in wildfire evacuations
Human-driven climate change increased the likelihood of Canada's 2023 record fire season
